canonical_summary:
Space compliance in India is built on a four-part regulatory stack that came together between 2020 and 2024. The Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre (IN-SPACe), constituted in 2021 and headquartered at Bopal, Ahmedabad since 10 June 2022, is the single-window authoriser for every non-government space activity in the country. It sits inside the Department of Space (DOS), works with the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) for technical diligence, and routes spectrum and orbital filings through the Wireless Planning and Coordination (WPC) wing of the Department of Telecommunications.

The operating manual for this stack is the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures for Implementation of the Indian Space Policy 2023 in respect of Authorization of Space Activities (NGP, document IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0), notified on 3 May 2024. It defines six authorisation categories, sets a 75 to 120 day clock from receipt of a complete application, and binds operators to debris mitigation, third-party liability insurance, and ongoing disclosure to IN-SPACe.

The Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) framework was rewritten by DPIIT Press Note 1 (2024 Series) on 4 March 2024 and brought into force through the FEMA Non-debt Instruments (Third Amendment) Rules on 16 April 2024. It carves space into three activity tiers with automatic-route thresholds of 74 percent, 49 percent, and 100 percent. Together, IN-SPACe, the NGP, the FDI reform, WPC coordination, and ISRO's Debris-Free Space Missions 2030 commitment form the complete space compliance regime an Indian or foreign operator must navigate.
h1: Space compliance in India: the IN-SPACe authorisation guide
meta_title: Space compliance in India and IN-SPACe authorisation
meta_description: A complete guide to space compliance in India: IN-SPACe authorisation, the NGP 2024 categories, FDI rules under Press Note 1, WPC and ITU coordination, and debris norms.

sections.heading: The Indian space regulatory stack: IN-SPACe, ISRO, DOS, and WPC
paragraphs: India's space sector is governed by four distinct institutions that each own a specific layer of the compliance stack. The Department of Space (DOS) is the sovereign parent department under the Government of India and holds the state-level obligations under the Outer Space Treaty, the Liability Convention, and the Registration Convention. It is also the department within which IN-SPACe and ISRO sit.

The Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre (IN-SPACe) is the autonomous, single-window regulator and authoriser. Its creation was announced by the Union Cabinet in June 2020, it was formally constituted in 2021, and its headquarters at Bopal, Ahmedabad was inaugurated by Prime Minister Narendra Modi on 10 June 2022. The current Chairman is Dr. Pawan Kumar Goenka. IN-SPACe is responsible to promote, enable, authorise, and supervise the space activities of Non-Government Entities (NGEs).

The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) is the research, development, and operations agency. Under the NGP 2024 framework, ISRO provides technical inputs and diligence support to IN-SPACe, including mission analysis, launch interfacing, and safety review, but it is no longer the authoriser. NewSpace India Limited (NSIL) is the commercial arm that executes public-private partnerships and demand-driven missions, and is not part of the authorisation chain.

The Wireless Planning and Coordination (WPC) wing of the Department of Telecommunications is India's National Radio Regulatory Authority and the sole notifying administration for India at the International Telecommunication Union (ITU). Every satellite filing, spectrum assignment, and earth-station siting clearance flows through WPC and its Standing Advisory Committee on Radio Frequency Allocation (SACFA).
bullets: DOS: sovereign state functions under UN space treaties and the National Registry of space objects.
IN-SPACe: single-window authoriser for NGEs, headquartered at Bopal, Ahmedabad since 10 June 2022.
ISRO: technical diligence, mission analysis, and launch interfacing in support of IN-SPACe.
WPC: ITU filings, NFAP, SACFA siting clearance, and frequency assignment letters.

sections.heading: Indian Space Policy 2023 and the NGP 2024 authorisation categories
paragraphs: The Indian Space Policy 2023, approved by the Cabinet on 6 April 2023, formalised IN-SPACe's role as the single authorising authority for space activities in India. To operationalise the policy, IN-SPACe notified the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures for Implementation of the Indian Space Policy 2023 in respect of Authorization of Space Activities (NGP, document IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0) on 3 May 2024.
The NGP defines six authorisation categories. It also empowers IN-SPACe to add further categories as the sector matures.
bullets: Category 1: establishment and operation of space objects, including GSO and NGSO communication satellites, remote sensing satellites, amateur satellites, and hosted payloads.
Category 2: operation of space transportation systems, including sub-orbital and orbital launches conducted from India or from outside India, and the setting up of spaceports.

Category 3: establishment and operation of ground systems, including Satellite Control Centres, TT&C stations, Mission Control Centres, remote sensing data reception stations, and Space Situational Awareness ground stations.
Category 4: dissemination of Earth observation and remote sensing data, with a specific compliance regime for high-resolution data finer than 30 cm GSD at nadir.
Category 5: sale or transfer of space objects in orbit, including in-orbit ownership change, control transfer, and end-of-life arrangements.
Category 6: re-entry plans, covering controlled and uncontrolled re-entry, debris mitigation, and end-of-mission disposal.

sections.heading: The IN-SPACe authorisation process, step by step
paragraphs: The NGP commits IN-SPACe to complete authorisation within 75 to 120 days from receipt of a complete application, inclusive of inter-ministerial and inter-departmental consultations. The Department of Space advises applicants to initiate the process at least six months before the intended commencement of the space activity.

Only Indian entities, governmental or non-governmental, may apply directly. A non-Indian entity may operate within Indian territory only through an Indian entity. Post authorisation, the operator carries continuing obligations: anomaly and material-change reporting within 24 to 48 hours for specified incidents, change-of-control or breach notices within 7 days, and ongoing disclosure of operational status to IN-SPACe.

subsections.heading: Pre-application
bullets: Confirm Indian entity status and the activity category under the NGP.
Identify whether existing Indian Orbital Resources will be used or a new ITU filing is required.
Engage WPC early to scope frequency, SACFA siting, and ITU API requirements.
Initiate the process at least six months ahead of intended operations.

subsections.heading: Application and diligence
bullets: Submit the application via the IN-SPACe portal with technical, financial, and security disclosures.
Demonstrate financial and technical capability to execute and sustain the mission.
Provide ITU filing status for TT&C, sensor, and communication payload frequencies.
Provide debris mitigation, end-of-mission disposal, and third-party liability insurance commitments.
Undergo inter-ministerial consultation including DOS, DoT, MHA, and ISRO technical review.

subsections.heading: Authorisation and post-authorisation
bullets: Receive authorisation within the 75 to 120 day window for a complete application.
For space objects, IN-SPACe recommends entry in the National Registry maintained by DOS under the Registration Convention.
Maintain mandatory third-party liability insurance throughout operations.
Report anomalies and material changes within 24 to 48 hours; report change-of-control or breach within 7 days.
Comply with debris mitigation, post-mission disposal, and coordination obligations through the mission lifecycle.

sections.heading: FDI rules for foreign investors in the Indian space sector
paragraphs: On 21 February 2024, the Union Cabinet approved an amendment to India's Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) policy for the space sector. The Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) issued Press Note 1 (2024 Series) on 4 March 2024 amending Paragraph 5.2.12 of the Consolidated FDI Policy Circular of 2020. The amendment took legal effect when the Ministry of Finance notified the Foreign Exchange Management (Non-debt Instruments) (Third Amendment) Rules, 2024 on 16 April 2024.

Before the reform, satellite establishment and operation required 100 percent FDI under the government approval route. The 2024 reform retains a 100 percent sectoral cap across all activities but creates a three-tier structure with graded automatic-route thresholds.

subsections.heading: Tier 1: satellites, ground segment, user segment, and satellite data products
bullets: Sectoral cap 100 percent.
Up to 74 percent under the automatic route.
Beyond 74 percent and up to 100 percent requires prior government approval.

subsections.heading: Tier 2: launch vehicles, associated systems and sub-systems, and spaceports
bullets: Sectoral cap 100 percent.
Up to 49 percent under the automatic route.
Beyond 49 percent and up to 100 percent requires prior government approval.

subsections.heading: Tier 3: components and sub-systems for satellites, ground segment, and user segment
bullets: Sectoral cap 100 percent under the automatic route.
No government-approval threshold.
Designed to plug Indian suppliers into global space supply chains.

subsections.heading: Overlay conditions
bullets: All FDI remains subject to DOS and IN-SPACe sectoral guidelines and to licensing and security clearance.

Press Note 3 (2020) continues to apply: any investment from a land-bordering country, or with beneficial ownership from such a country, must use the government route regardless of the sectoral automatic threshold.

sections.heading: ITU, WPC, and spectrum coordination for Indian satellites
paragraphs: WPC, created in 1952, is the National Radio Regulatory Authority of India under the Indian Telegraph Act 1885 and the Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933. It is also the sole notifying administration for India at the ITU Radiocommunication Bureau in Geneva, and the national nodal agency for the Asia-Pacific Telecommunity on radio frequency management and satellite orbital resources.

An Indian operator authorised by IN-SPACe must coordinate with WPC to access Indian Orbital Resources or to make a new ITU filing. The lifecycle moves through three ITU stages, with the National Frequency Allocation Plan (NFAP) underpinning every domestic assignment.

subsections.heading: ITU filing lifecycle
bullets: Advance Publication Information (API) under RR No. 9.1 or 9.2, published in the BR International Frequency Information Circular (BR IFIC).
Coordination Request (CR/C) under RR No. 9.7 for networks subject to coordination, filed via the ITU e-Submission system.
Notification and recording in the Master International Frequency Register (MIFR) under Article 11, with a seven-year regulatory deadline to bring the network into use.

subsections.heading: Domestic spectrum stack
bullets: NFAP-2022 mirrors the ITU Radio Regulations 2020 and covers up to 3000 GHz.
NFAP-2025, notified by DoT, takes effect from 30 December 2025 to align with post-WRC-23 standards.

The Telecommunications Act 2023, Section 4 with the First Schedule, lists satellite-based services (GMPCS, VSAT, NLD/ILD satellite, IFMC, broadcasting satellite, and gateway earth station spectrum) for administrative assignment rather than auction.
Provisional satellite spectrum assignment was operationalised by the DoT notification dated 10 October 2024, conditional on a DoT service licence and IN-SPACe authorisation.
SACFA siting clearance and a Frequency Assignment Letter from WPC are required before any earth station, VSAT hub, or microwave link is brought into use.
The Wireless Monitoring Organisation (WMO), operating the International Monitoring Station at Ranchi, handles interference investigations and ITU Article 15 escalations.

sections.heading: Debris mitigation requirements and ISRO's IS4OM regime
paragraphs: ISRO's institutional capability for orbital debris and Space Situational Awareness (SSA) is anchored in IS4OM, the ISRO System for Safe and Sustainable Space Operations Management, at the ISTRAC campus in Peenya, Bengaluru. IS4OM houses ISRO's debris research, modelling, and SSA Control Centre functions and runs Collision Avoidance Analysis (COLA) for every Indian launch from Sriharikota.

The Indian Space Situational Assessment Report 2024 records that as of 31 December 2024 India operated 22 satellites in LEO and 31 in GEO, plus Chandrayaan-2 Orbiter and Aditya-L1, with 136 Indian spacecraft launched cumulatively. During 2024 IS4OM screened more than 53,000 conjunction alerts from the 18th Space Defense Squadron of USSPACECOM, executed 10 Collision Avoidance Manoeuvres (6 in LEO, 4 in GEO, 1 deep space), and revised 89 manoeuvre plans. Scatsat-1 was de-orbited over 12 manoeuvres with full fuel depletion and passivation.

subsections.heading: Debris-Free Space Missions (DFSM) 2030
paragraphs: DFSM was announced by then-Chairman S. Somanath at the 42nd Inter-Agency Space Debris Coordination Committee (IADC) annual meeting in Bengaluru on 16 April 2024. It commits all Indian governmental and non-governmental space actors to debris-free operations by 2030. ISRO chaired the IADC for 2023-24 and currently chairs the UN COPUOS Working Group on the Long-term Sustainability of Outer Space Activities.
bullets: Post-mission disposal of spent stages and satellites with greater than 99 percent success probability.
Residual orbital lifetime capped at 5 years for LEO missions.
Mandatory trackability, identifiability, and manoeuvrability through the mission lifecycle.
A protected band of 400 km plus or minus 30 km reserved for human spaceflight.

subsections.heading: Operator obligations under the NGP
bullets: Submit debris mitigation and end-of-mission disposal plans at the time of authorisation.
Carry mandatory third-party liability insurance.
Coordinate conjunction data with IS4OM and execute CAMs when conjunction risk crosses threshold.
Report re-entry plans (Category 6 of the NGP) covering controlled and uncontrolled re-entry.

sections.heading: What foreign operators need to know
paragraphs: Foreign entities cannot apply directly to IN-SPACe. They access the Indian market through an Indian entity, typically a joint venture or subsidiary, and the Indian entity is the named applicant under the NGP. The 2024 FDI reform sets the equity ceilings for that Indian entity, and the IN-SPACe authorisation, DoT service licence, and WPC spectrum assignment are independent regulatory checkpoints that must each be cleared.
bullets: Foreign satcom operators serving Indian users had a transition grace period running up to 31 March 2025 to obtain IN-SPACe authorisation under the NGP.
Hold or build an Indian entity: only Indian entities can hold IN-SPACe authorisation under the NGP.
Pick the right FDI tier: 74 percent automatic for satellites and ground systems, 49 percent automatic for launch vehicles and spaceports, 100 percent automatic for components.
Plan for Press Note 3 (2020): investors from land-bordering countries, or with beneficial ownership in such countries, take the government route regardless of sector.
Initiate the application at least six months before intended operations and expect a 75 to 120 day clock from a complete application.
For communications missions, file ITU API, CR/C, and MIFR notification through WPC, and budget for the seven-year bring-into-use deadline.
Obtain SACFA siting clearance and a WPC Frequency Assignment Letter before bringing earth stations or gateways into use.

Comply with DFSM 2030: 99 percent post-mission disposal success, 5-year LEO residual lifetime, mandatory trackability and manoeuvrability, and the 400 km plus or minus 30 km protected human spaceflight band.
Carry mandatory third-party liability insurance and accept post-authorisation reporting duties: 24 to 48 hours for anomalies, 7 days for change of control.

faqs.question: What is IN-SPACe and what does it regulate?
faqs.answer: The Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre (IN-SPACe) is the autonomous, single-window nodal agency for space activities in India. It sits within the Department of Space, was constituted in 2021, and operates from a dedicated headquarters at Bopal, Ahmedabad inaugurated on 10 June 2022. Under the Indian Space Policy 2023 and the NGP 2024, IN-SPACe is the sole authoriser for non-government entities operating space objects, space transportation systems, ground systems, Earth observation data dissemination, in-orbit transfers, and re-entry. It also promotes and supervises space activity and shares ISRO facilities with the private sector.

faqs.question: Which authorisation categories are defined under the NGP 2024?
faqs.answer: The Norms, Guidelines and Procedures for Implementation of the Indian Space Policy 2023 in respect of Authorization of Space Activities (document IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0), notified on 3 May 2024, defines six categories. They are: establishment and operation of space objects, including GSO and NGSO satellites; operation of space transportation systems and spaceports; establishment and operation of ground systems; dissemination of Earth observation data with a specific regime for resolution finer than 30 cm GSD at nadir; sale or transfer of space objects in orbit; and re-entry plans. IN-SPACe can add categories as the sector matures.

faqs.question: How long does IN-SPACe authorisation take?
faqs.answer: The NGP commits IN-SPACe to complete authorisation within 75 to 120 days from receipt of a complete application, inclusive of all inter-ministerial and inter-departmental consultations. The Department of Space advises applicants to initiate the process at least six months before the intended commencement of the space activity to absorb clarification cycles, ITU coordination dependencies, and security clearances. Post authorisation, operators carry shorter reporting clocks: 24 to 48 hours for specified anomalies, and 7 days for change-of-control or material breach notices. Foreign satcom operators serving India had until 31 March 2025 to come under authorisation.

faqs.question: What are the FDI limits for the Indian space sector?
faqs.answer: DPIIT Press Note 1 (2024 Series), issued on 4 March 2024 and effective through the Foreign Exchange Management (Non-debt Instruments) (Third Amendment) Rules notified on 16 April 2024, sets a three-tier structure. Satellites, ground segment, user segment, and satellite data products take 74 percent automatic and the balance up to 100 percent under government approval. Launch vehicles, associated systems, and spaceports take 49 percent automatic and the balance up to 100 percent under approval. Components and sub-systems for satellites, ground and user segments take 100 percent automatic with no approval threshold. All tiers remain subject to DOS and IN-SPACe guidelines.

faqs.question: Can a foreign company directly apply to IN-SPACe?
faqs.answer: No. Under the NGP 2024, only Indian entities, governmental or non-governmental, may apply directly. A non-Indian entity can operate within Indian territory only through an Indian entity that holds the authorisation. Foreign investors therefore typically build the Indian entity first, using one of the three FDI tiers set by DPIIT Press Note 1 (2024 Series), and the Indian entity is the named applicant. Investors covered by Press Note 3 (2020), specifically those from land-bordering countries or with beneficial ownership in such countries, must take the government route regardless of the sectoral automatic threshold.

faqs.question: How does ITU filing through WPC work for an Indian satellite?
faqs.answer: The Wireless Planning and Coordination wing of the Department of Telecommunications is India's sole notifying administration at the ITU Radiocommunication Bureau. An Indian operator authorised by IN-SPACe routes filings through WPC in three stages: Advance Publication Information under RR No. 9.1 or 9.2, published in the BR International Frequency Information Circular; Coordination Request under RR No. 9.7 for networks subject to coordination, lodged via ITU e-Submission; and notification and recording in the Master International Frequency Register under Article 11. The network must be brought into use within the seven-year regulatory deadline.

faqs.question: What spectrum and licensing approvals are needed alongside IN-SPACe authorisation?
faqs.answer: Spectrum is administered by WPC under the Indian Telegraph Act 1885 and the Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933, and is allocated against the National Frequency Allocation Plan (NFAP 2022, with NFAP 2025 effective from 30 December 2025). Section 4 and the First Schedule of the Telecommunications Act 2023 list satellite-based services for administrative assignment, and the DoT notification of 10 October 2024 operationalised provisional satellite spectrum assignment conditional on a DoT service licence and IN-SPACe authorisation. Earth stations also require SACFA siting clearance and a WPC Frequency Assignment Letter before commissioning.

faqs.question: What debris mitigation rules apply to Indian space missions?
faqs.answer: ISRO's Debris-Free Space Missions initiative, announced on 16 April 2024 at the 42nd IADC annual meeting in Bengaluru, commits all Indian space actors to debris-free operations by 2030. The quantified requirements are post-mission disposal with greater than 99 percent success probability, residual orbital lifetime capped at 5 years for LEO missions, mandatory trackability, identifiability and manoeuvrability through the mission lifecycle, and a protected band of 400 km plus or minus 30 km for human spaceflight. IS4OM at ISTRAC, Bengaluru runs Collision Avoidance Analysis and screens conjunction alerts from the 18th Space Defense Squadron.

faqs.question: What does IS4OM do for an operator?
faqs.answer: IS4OM, the ISRO System for Safe and Sustainable Space Operations Management, is the operational Space Situational Awareness centre at the ISTRAC campus in Peenya, Bengaluru. It performs orbit determination and cataloguing, COLA for every Indian launch from Sriharikota, continual close-approach assessment for operational satellites, and Collision Avoidance Manoeuvre recommendations when conjunction risk crosses threshold. During 2024 IS4OM screened more than 53,000 conjunction alerts, executed 10 CAMs (6 in LEO, 4 in GEO, 1 deep space) and revised 89 manoeuvre plans. It also coordinates end-of-life de-orbit, including the 12-manoeuvre Scatsat-1 disposal.

faqs.question: Is third-party liability insurance mandatory under Indian space rules?
faqs.answer: Yes. The NGP 2024 imposes mandatory third-party liability insurance on authorised entities as a standing post-authorisation obligation, alongside demonstration of financial and technical capability, adherence to safety and security protocols, space debris and environmental mitigation, and continuous disclosure of operational status to IN-SPACe. The insurance requirement reflects India's exposure as the launching state under the 1972 Liability Convention, for which the Department of Space holds the state-level responsibility. Operators must therefore size their cover to mission profile, orbital regime, third-party exposure on launch and re-entry, and the specific risk envelope IN-SPACe accepts at authorisation.

faqs.question: How do the Indian Space Policy 2023 and the NGP 2024 fit together?
faqs.answer: The Indian Space Policy 2023, approved by the Cabinet on 6 April 2023, is the framing document. It defines the roles of DOS, ISRO, IN-SPACe, and NSIL, opens the sector to non-government entities, and designates IN-SPACe as the single authorising authority. The NGP 2024, notified on 3 May 2024 as document IN:ISP2023:NGP2024/V1.0, is the operating manual that translates the policy into binding application criteria, six authorisation categories, 75 to 120 day timelines, debris mitigation duties, insurance requirements, and reporting obligations. Together they form the legal bedrock for Indian and foreign-backed space operators.

faqs.question: What is the role of ISRO under the new compliance regime?
faqs.answer: ISRO is no longer the authoriser for non-government space activity. Under the NGP 2024 it provides technical inputs and diligence support to IN-SPACe, including mission analysis, launch interfacing, and safety review, and it continues to run R&D, operational launches from Sriharikota, and the IS4OM SSA function at ISTRAC, Bengaluru. The Department of Space holds sovereign state functions under the UN space treaties, maintains the National Registry of space objects to which IN-SPACe recommends entries, and remains the state party responsible for India's obligations under the Outer Space Treaty and the Liability Convention.
